content.full_text:
R AV I M H H
FAIR POLITICALPRACTICES COMMISSION
March 7,
1996
Doreen Liberto Blanck
370 Weymouth Street
Cambria, California
93428
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No.
1—96—081
Dear Ms.
Blanck:
This is in response to your request for advice regarding the
conflict—of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act
(the
“Act”) •1
OUESTION
You are the director of the community development department.
Will you have a conflict of interest if you purchase a house from
an architect who submits architectural plans to the community
development department?
CONCLUS ION
You will not have a conflict unless the architect becomes a
source of income to you.
If the architect becomes a source of
income to you,
you must disqualify yourself from any governmental
decision which will have a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on the architect.
FACTS
You are a community development director for a small city.
Last year,
your department processed plans for a property owner.
The property owner’s architect was involved in a few meetings on
the project, but approval of the project was delayed for a variety
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations,
Sections
18000—18995.
428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814
(916) 322-5660


File No.
1—96—081
Page
2
of reasons.
A staff member informed you that the property owner
felt that the architect was delaying the project by not making the
required changes and, if this continued,
some legal action might
be taken.
The project is now under construction and is being
monitored by another department which is not under your
supervision.
You are considering making an offer on a house owned by this
architect located outside your department’s jurisdiction.
The
offer could include using your existing home as a down payment or
exchange and financing the remaining amount through a financial
institution.
APPLICABLE LW
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official from
making, participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision in which
the official has a financial interest.
Section 87103 specifies that a public official has a
financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect,
distinguishable from the effect on the public generally,
on the
official or---a--member of his or her immediate family or on:
***
(b)
Any real property in which the public official
has a direct or indirect interest worth one thousand
dollars
($1,000)
or more.
(c)
Any source of income,
other than gifts and
other than loans by a commercial lending institution in
the regular course of business on terms available to the
public without regard to official status,
aggregating
two hundred fifty dollars
($250)
or more in value
provided to,
received by or promised to the public
official within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.
***
(e)
Any donor of,
or any intermediary or agent for
a donor of,
a gift or gifts aggregating two hundred
fifty dollars
($250) or more in value provided to,
received by,
or promised to the public official within
1
months prior to the time when the decision is made.
The amount of the value of gifts specified by this
subdivision shall be adjusted biennially by the
Commission to equal the same amount determined by the
Commission pursuant to subdivision
(d)
of Section 89504.
(Section 87103.)


File No.
1—96—081
Page
3
A public official is defined,
in relevant part,
as any
member, officer,
employee or consultant of a local government
agency.
(Section 82048.)
A public official makes or participates in a governmental
decision when he or she does such actions as voting on a matter,
appointing a person,
committing his or her agency to any course of
action,
enters into a contractual agreement on behalf of his or
her agency,
and advises or makes recommendations to a
decisionmaker without substantive review.
(Regulation 18700(b)-
(c).)
A public official influences a governmental decision when
he or she “contacts,
or appears before,
or otherwise attempts to
influence any member, officer,
employee or consultant of the
agency.”
(Regulation 18700.1.)
The definition of
“income” does not include a loan from a
commercial lending institution “made in the lender’s regular
course of business on terms available to members of the public
without regard to official status if:
(A)
[u]sed to purchase,
refinance the purchase of,
or for improvements to, the principal
residence of filer...”
(Section 82030(b) (8).)
A “gift” to a public official is “any payment to the extent
that consideration of equal or greater value is not received.”
(Section 82028.)
A discount on the sale of goods to a public
official may be a gift from the seller if the discount is based on
the buyer’s official status.
(Aleshire Advice Letter,
No.
1-90-
641.)
ANALYSIS
You are the community development director for a small city
and,
as such,
are a “public official” under the Act.
(Section
82048.)
You plan to purchase a new home and sell your current home.
If a public official sells a real property interest,
or any other
item,
for more than $250, the purchaser becomes a source of income
to the official.
(Katz Advice Letter,
No.
84-112.)
Currently, the architect from whom you plan to purchase a new
home is not a source of income to you.
If you simply purchase the
home from the architect financed by a loan from a conuuercial
lending institution, the architect will not become a source of
income to you.
There are some ways,
however,
in which the architect may
become a source of income to you if you purchase the house with
alternative financing.
For instance,
you may negotiate a seller
financed purchase with the architect whereby the architect loans
you the money to purchase the house directly.
Any loan received
from the architect,
who is not a commercial lender, would be
income to you.
Similarly, if you arrange to trade your house to
the architect as part of the purchase price,
the architect would


File No.
1—96—081
Page
4
in effect purchase your house and thus become a source of income
to you.
(Maloney Advice Letter,
No.
A-84-130.)
You mentioned that you may purchase the architect’s house for
less than the current asking price.
So long as any discount on
the purchase price is not based on your official status and is
negotiated by you as a member of the public,
any difference
between the asking price and the price paid will not be considered
a gift to you from the architect.
(Section 82028; Aleshire Advice
Letter, supra.)
If the architect becomes a source of income to you,
you may
not make or participate in making any governmental decision which
will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on
the architect.
You mentioned that you may be called to testify in
court if the architect is sued by his or her client.
Any
testimony you give under oath as part of a legal proceeding will
not be considered participating in,
making or influencing a
governmental decision.
Accordingly,
you may testify in a legal
proceeding pursuant to subpoena even if the architect becomes a
source of income to you.
2
If you have any further questions, please feel free to
contact me at
(916)
322—5660.
Sincerely,
Steven C.
Churchwell
General Counsel
-
By:
Liane Randolph
Counsel,
Legal Division
SGC:LR:ak
2
WIiether a governmental decision will have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect is a factual question which
must be analyzed on a case—by—case basis.
If the architect
becomes a source of income to you,
you may want to seek further
advice to determine if particular decisions will have a material
financial effect on the architect.

decision which will have a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on the architect.

QUESTION: You are the director of the community development department.
Will you have a conflict of interest if you purchase a house from
an architect who submits architectural plans to the community
development department?

CONCLUSION: You will not have a conflict unless the architect becomes a
source of income to you.
If the architect becomes a source of
income to you,
you must disqualify yourself from any governmental
decision which will have a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on the architect.
